metadata.notes: Atlas content externalized from the original single-file renderer for reproducible dataset versioning.

nodes.label: Being
nodes.desc: The bedrock of ontology — that something exists rather than nothing. Heidegger called it the forgotten question of Western thought.
nodes.ref: Heidegger, Being and Time (1927); Parfit, Reasons and Persons (1984)

nodes.label: Truth
nodes.desc: The correspondence, coherence, or pragmatic usefulness of a belief. Recent epistemology interrogates how social forces distort what counts as truth.
nodes.ref: James, Pragmatism (1907); Fricker, Epistemic Injustice (2007)

nodes.label: Free Will
nodes.desc: Whether deliberation is genuinely open or secretly determined. Compatibilists argue freedom is compatible with determinism; libertarians deny it.
nodes.ref: Kane, The Significance of Free Will (1996); Dennett, Freedom Evolves (2003)

nodes.label: Consciousness
nodes.desc: Chalmers's "hard problem" — why physical processes give rise to subjective experience at all. The central mystery of philosophy of mind.
nodes.ref: Chalmers, The Conscious Mind (1996); Nagel, What Is It Like to Be a Bat? (1974)

nodes.label: Time
nodes.desc: Whether time flows or is a static block universe. Physicists and philosophers remain split on whether the past and future are equally real.
nodes.ref: McTaggart, The Unreality of Time (1908); Barbour, The End of Time (1999)

nodes.label: Causality
nodes.desc: The structure of influence: does cause genuinely produce effect, or is it a pattern we impose on correlated events?
nodes.ref: Hume, Enquiry (1748); Pearl, The Book of Why (2018)

nodes.label: Emergence
nodes.desc: How high-level properties — wetness, life, mind — arise from lower-level parts without being reducible to them.
nodes.ref: Anderson, More Is Different (1972); Deacon, Incomplete Nature (2011)

nodes.label: Phenomenology
nodes.desc: The first-person study of how things appear to consciousness — bracketing assumptions to describe experience directly.
nodes.ref: Husserl, Ideas I (1913); Merleau-Ponty, Phenomenology of Perception (1945)

nodes.label: Determinism
nodes.desc: The thesis that every event, including every thought, is the inevitable result of prior causes and the laws of nature.
nodes.ref: Laplace, Philosophical Essay on Probabilities (1814)

nodes.label: Intentionality
nodes.desc: The "aboutness" of mental states — the fact that thoughts are always directed at something. A mark that distinguishes mind from mere matter.
nodes.ref: Brentano, Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874); Searle, Intentionality (1983)

nodes.label: Paradox
nodes.desc: Two incompatible conclusions, each apparently valid. Paradoxes drive progress in logic, mathematics, and physics.
nodes.ref: Russell's Paradox (1901); Zeno's Paradoxes; Quine, The Ways of Paradox (1966)

nodes.label: Love
nodes.desc: Neuroscientifically, love activates reward circuits, suppresses critical social judgment, and releases oxytocin — bonding us across selves.
nodes.ref: Fisher, Why We Love (2004); Zeki, The Neurobiology of Love (2007)

nodes.label: Grief
nodes.desc: A non-linear neurobiological process: the brain's attempt to update a predictive model that still expects the deceased to exist.
nodes.ref: O'Connor, Grieving (2022 Nature Reviews Neuroscience)

nodes.label: Wonder
nodes.desc: Awe at scale or complexity. Research shows awe measurably shrinks the self-concept and expands social concern — a reset button for the ego.
nodes.ref: Keltner & Haidt, Approaching Awe (2003); Stellar et al., Self-Transcendent Emotions (2017)

nodes.label: Fear
nodes.desc: Mediated primarily by the amygdala, fear evolved as a fast-route survival signal — bypassing cortical deliberation in milliseconds.
nodes.ref: LeDoux, The Emotional Brain (1996); Ohman, Fear and Anxiety (2000)

nodes.label: Joy
nodes.desc: A state of elevated dopaminergic and serotonergic activity. Barbara Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory: joy expands thought-action repertoires.
nodes.ref: Fredrickson, The Broaden-and-Build Theory (2001)

nodes.label: Loneliness
nodes.desc: Chroni loneliness is now classified as a public health crisis. It activates the same neural pain circuits as physical injury — isolation literally hurts.
nodes.ref: Cacioppo & Patrick, Loneliness (2008); Holt-Lunstad et al., Social Isolation (2015)

nodes.label: Empathy
nodes.desc: Both affective (feeling with) and cognitive (perspective-taking). Mirror neuron systems partially underpin it — but cultural learning is equally decisive.
nodes.ref: Decety & Jackson, The Functional Architecture of Empathy (2004); Singer & Klimecki (2014)

nodes.label: Shame
nodes.desc: A global negative evaluation of the self — distinct from guilt (about acts). Chronic shame is linked to narcissism, depression, and aggression.
nodes.ref: Tangney & Dearing, Shame and Guilt (2002); Brown, I Thought It Was Just Me (2007)

nodes.label: Awe
nodes.desc: Triggered by vastness that challenges existing mental schemas. Awe reduces inflammation markers and increases prosocial behavior.
nodes.ref: Stellar et al., Awe and Inflammation (2015, Emotion)

nodes.label: Nostalgia
nodes.desc: A bittersweet emotion that boosts social connectedness and meaning. Neuroimaging shows it recruits autobiographical memory and reward systems together.
nodes.ref: Wildschut et al., Nostalgia: Content, Triggers, Functions (2006)

nodes.label: Predictive Processing
nodes.desc: The leading theory of brain function: the brain is a prediction machine, constantly generating models of the world and updating on prediction errors. Perception is controlled hallucination.
nodes.ref: Clark, Surfing Uncertainty (2016); Friston, The Free-Energy Principle (2010)

nodes.label: Default Mode Network
nodes.desc: A brain network active during rest, self-referential thought, and social cognition. Dysregulation links to depression, anxiety, and rumination.
nodes.ref: Raichle, The Brain's Default Mode Network (2015, Annual Review)

nodes.label: Neuroplasticity
nodes.desc: The brain's lifelong capacity to rewire synaptic connections in response to experience — overturning the once-dominant view that adult brains are fixed.
nodes.ref: Doidge, The Brain That Changes Itself (2007); Merzenich, Soft-Wired (2013)

nodes.label: Interoception
nodes.desc: The brain's sensing of the body's internal state — heartbeat, gut, breath. Increasingly seen as the biological root of emotion, self, and even social awareness.
nodes.ref: Craig, How Do You Feel? (2015); Seth, Being You (2021)

nodes.label: Mirror Neurons
nodes.desc: Neurons that fire both when executing an action and when observing another perform it. Proposed as a substrate for imitation, empathy, and language acquisition.
nodes.ref: Rizzolatti & Sinigaglia, Mirrors in the Brain (2008)

nodes.label: Synaptic Pruning
nodes.desc: The adolescent brain eliminates unused synaptic connections — a process of neural editing that shapes personality and vulnerability to psychiatric conditions.
nodes.ref: Paus, Keshavan & Giedd, Why Do Many Psychiatric Disorders Emerge in Adolescence? (2008)

nodes.label: Embodied Cognition
nodes.desc: Cognition is not computation in a brain-box but is shaped by the whole body and environment. Posture, gesture, and sensation co-constitute thought.
nodes.ref: Varela, Thompson & Rosch, The Embodied Mind (1991); Lakoff & Johnson, Philosophy in the Flesh (1999)

nodes.label: Attention
nodes.desc: The selective allocation of neural resources. William James called it "the taking possession by the mind." Its voluntary control is the foundation of self-regulation.
nodes.ref: Posner, Orienting of Attention (1980); McGilchrist, The Master and His Emissary (2009)

nodes.label: Sleep & Memory
nodes.desc: During sleep, the hippocampus replays waking experiences and transfers them to cortical long-term storage — memory consolidation requires unconsciousness.
nodes.ref: Walker, Why We Sleep (2017); Wilson & McNaughton (1994, Science)

nodes.label: Emotional Regulation
nodes.desc: The set of strategies — reappraisal, suppression, distancing — by which the prefrontal cortex modulates amygdalar responses to emotional stimuli.
nodes.ref: Gross, The Emerging Field of Emotion Regulation (1998)

nodes.label: Salience
nodes.desc: The salience network (insula + anterior cingulate) determines what reaches conscious awareness — it is the gatekeeper between internal and external worlds.
nodes.ref: Menon & Uddin, Saliency, Switching, Attention (2010)

nodes.label: Glymphatic System
nodes.desc: The brain's waste-clearance system — active during deep sleep — flushes out amyloid and tau proteins implicated in Alzheimer's disease.
nodes.ref: Iliff et al. (2012, Science Translational Medicine)

nodes.label: Entropy
nodes.desc: The universe's tendency toward disorder defines the arrow of time. Boltzmann showed entropy is a measure of the number of microscopic arrangements a system can occupy.
nodes.ref: Boltzmann (1872); Carroll, From Eternity to Here (2010)

nodes.label: Quantum Superposition
nodes.desc: A quantum system exists in multiple states simultaneously until measured. Schrödinger's equation governs superposed states — its interpretation remains contested.
nodes.ref: Schrödinger (1935); Everett, Relative State Formulation (1957)

nodes.label: Infinity
nodes.desc: Cantor's set theory revealed infinite hierarchies of infinity. In physics, infinities in equations often signal the limits of current theory.
nodes.ref: Cantor, Über unendliche Lineare Punktmannigfaltigkeiten (1883)

nodes.label: Information Theory
nodes.desc: Shannon's formalization of information as a measure of surprise. Modern physics increasingly treats information as fundamental — "it from bit."
nodes.ref: Shannon, A Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948); Wheeler (1990)

nodes.label: Many-Worlds
nodes.desc: Everett's interpretation of quantum mechanics: every quantum event branches the universe into parallel outcomes, all equally real.
nodes.ref: Everett (1957); Deutsch, The Fabric of Reality (1997)

nodes.label: Complexity Theory
nodes.desc: The science of systems with many interacting parts that produce emergent, unpredictable global behaviors — from ant colonies to economies to consciousness.
nodes.ref: Holland, Emergence: From Chaos to Order (1998); Kauffman, The Origins of Order (1993)

nodes.label: Dark Matter
nodes.desc: Unknown matter comprising 27% of the universe — inferred from gravitational effects but never directly detected. The universe is mostly invisible to us.
nodes.ref: Rubin & Ford (1970); Planck Collaboration (2018)

nodes.label: Holographic Principle
nodes.desc: All information in a volume of space can be encoded on its boundary surface — suggesting our three-dimensional universe may be a projection.
nodes.ref: 't Hooft (1993); Susskind (1995); Maldacena, The Large-N Limit (1997)

nodes.label: Metacognition
nodes.desc: Thinking about thinking. The ability to monitor and regulate one's own cognitive processes — a key differentiator of expert from novice performance.
nodes.ref: Flavell, Metacognition and Cognitive Monitoring (1979); Dunning-Kruger (1999)

nodes.label: Cognitive Bias
nodes.desc: Systematic errors in reasoning arising from mental shortcuts (heuristics). Kahneman and Tversky catalogued over 180 — they are not bugs but evolved features.
nodes.ref: Tversky & Kahneman, Judgment Under Uncertainty (1974); Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011)

nodes.label: Flow State
nodes.desc: Csikszentmihalyi's optimal experience: total absorption in a skill-matched challenge, accompanied by loss of self-consciousness and distorted time perception.
nodes.ref: Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience (1990)

nodes.label: Theory of Mind
nodes.desc: The capacity to attribute mental states — beliefs, desires, intentions — to others. Absent in early autism; it underlies all social complexity and storytelling.
nodes.ref: Premack & Woodruff (1978); Baron-Cohen, Leslie & Frith (1985)

nodes.label: Working Memory
nodes.desc: The cognitive workspace where information is held and manipulated for immediate use. Baddeley's model identifies a phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and central executive.
nodes.ref: Baddeley & Hitch (1974); Miller, The Magical Number Seven (1956)

nodes.label: Pattern Recognition
nodes.desc: The brain's core operation — finding regularities in noisy sensory data. Both its power and its weakness: we find patterns even where none exist (apophenia).
nodes.ref: Gestalt psychology; Kahneman; Shermer, The Believing Brain (2011)

nodes.label: Decision Making
nodes.desc: Somatic marker hypothesis: emotion is not the enemy of reason but is necessary for rational choice. Patients with prefrontal damage can't decide despite intact logic.
nodes.ref: Damasio, Descartes' Error (1994); Kahneman & Tversky, Prospect Theory (1979)

nodes.label: Conceptual Metaphor
nodes.desc: Lakoff & Johnson: abstract concepts are structured by embodied metaphors. We understand argument as war, time as a resource, the mind as a container.
nodes.ref: Lakoff & Johnson, Metaphors We Live By (1980)

nodes.label: Linguistic Relativity
nodes.desc: The hypothesis that language shapes thought — weak version widely accepted: different languages predispose different cognitive patterns (color, time, space).
nodes.ref: Whorf (1940s); Boroditsky, How Language Shapes Thought (2011)

nodes.label: Cognitive Load
nodes.desc: Working memory has limited capacity — when overloaded, learning and decision quality collapse. Sweller's theory underpins modern instructional design.
nodes.ref: Sweller, Cognitive Load During Problem Solving (1988)

nodes.label: Beauty
nodes.desc: Kant's "free play of imagination and understanding." Neurostheticians find beauty activates the medial orbito-frontal cortex — a region associated with reward.
nodes.ref: Kant, Critique of Judgment (1790); Chatterjee & Vartanian, Neuroaesthetics (2014)

nodes.label: Story
nodes.desc: The mind's primary sense-making form. Narrative activates multiple brain regions simultaneously — motor, sensory, emotional — far more than factual statements.
nodes.ref: Bruner, Actual Minds, Possible Worlds (1986); Gottschall, The Storytelling Animal (2012)

nodes.label: Music
nodes.desc: Music uniquely activates reward, motor, and emotional circuits simultaneously. Anticipation — tension before resolution — is as pleasurable as the resolution itself.
nodes.ref: Huron, Sweet Anticipation (2006); Salimpoor et al., Anatomically Distinct Dopamine Release (2011)

nodes.label: Imagination
nodes.desc: Shares neural architecture with episodic memory and future simulation — the hippocampus is central to all three. To imagine is to remember a world that hasn't happened yet.
nodes.ref: Schacter & Addis, The Cognitive Neuroscience of Constructive Memory (2007)

nodes.label: Play
nodes.desc: Cross-species behavior that is intrinsically motivated, process-focused, and preparatory. In humans, play is the laboratory of innovation, identity, and social rule learning.
nodes.ref: Sutton-Smith, The Ambiguity of Play (1997); Brown, Play (2009)

nodes.label: Symbol
nodes.desc: The capacity to let one thing stand for another. Human symbolic thought — beginning with cave art ~45,000 ya — is uniquely open-ended and culturally cumulative.
nodes.ref: Deacon, The Symbolic Species (1997); Mithen, The Prehistory of the Mind (1996)

nodes.label: Ritual
nodes.desc: Repeated, symbolic action that coordinates collective emotion and reinforces group identity. Cross-culturally universal — from funerals to handshakes to sport.
nodes.ref: Bell, Ritual Theory, Ritual Practice (1992); Durkheim, The Elementary Forms of Religious Life (1912)

nodes.label: Identity
nodes.desc: Constructed, not discovered. Narrative identity theory: the self is the story we tell about the continuity of our experiences across time.
nodes.ref: Ricoeur, Oneself as Another (1992); McAdams, The Stories We Live By (1993)

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.desc: Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive — every act of recall rewrites the trace. Loftus's false memory research revolutionized eyewitness testimony law.
nodes.ref: Loftus, The Myth of Repressed Memory (1994); Schacter, The Seven Sins of Memory (2001)

nodes.label: Death
nodes.desc: Terror Management Theory: much of human culture is an anxiety buffer against awareness of mortality. Becker won the Pulitzer arguing civilizations are denial-of-death projects.
nodes.ref: Becker, The Denial of Death (1973); Greenberg, Solomon & Pyszczynski, TMT (1986)

nodes.label: Purpose
nodes.desc: Frankl showed purpose is foundational to psychological survival. Recent research links sense of purpose to reduced inflammation, longer telomeres, lower mortality.
nodes.ref: Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning (1946); Hill et al., Purpose in Life (2014)

nodes.label: Ego
nodes.desc: The constructed sense of being a unified, continuous self — now understood to be a brain-generated inference, not a fact about the world.
nodes.ref: Metzinger, Being No One (2003); Hood, The Self Illusion (2012)

nodes.label: Trauma
nodes.desc: Trauma encodes memories differently — fragmentary, sensory, easily re-triggered. Van der Kolk's work showed PTSD is literally stored in the body's physiology.
nodes.ref: Van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score (2014); Levine, Waking the Tiger (1997)

nodes.label: Dream
nodes.desc: REM dreams emerge when the PFC is offline and the limbic system is hyperactive. Activation-synthesis theory: dreams are the brain making narrative from random firing.
nodes.ref: Hobson & McCarley, The Brain as a Dream State Generator (1977); Walker (2017)

nodes.label: Attachment
nodes.desc: Bowlby's discovery: early caregiver bonds create internal working models that shape all subsequent relationships. Four attachment styles have been cross-culturally replicated.
nodes.ref: Bowlby, Attachment (1969); Ainsworth, Patterns of Attachment (1978)

nodes.label: Resilience
nodes.desc: Not the absence of trauma but the capacity to reorganize adaptive functioning under threat. Post-traumatic growth — becoming stronger at broken places — is well-documented.
nodes.ref: Bonanno, Loss, Trauma, and Human Resilience (2004); Tedeschi & Calhoun, PTG (1996)

nodes.label: Ego Death
nodes.desc: The dissolution of self-boundaries during psychedelic states or deep meditation. Correlated with decreased default mode network activity. Therapeutic potential now actively researched.
nodes.ref: Carhart-Harris et al., Neural Correlates of the Psychedelic State (2012)

nodes.label: Justice
nodes.desc: Rawls's veil of ignorance: principles chosen without knowing your place in society. Sen argues justice is inseparable from capabilities — what people can actually do and be.
nodes.ref: Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971); Sen, The Idea of Justice (2009)

nodes.label: Power
nodes.desc: Foucault's power is not possessed but exercised through discourse, institutions, and norms — it produces subjects as much as it represses them.
nodes.ref: Foucault, Discipline and Punish (1975); Lukes, Power: A Radical View (2005)

nodes.label: Trust
nodes.desc: The foundation of social capital and economic exchange. High-trust societies outperform low-trust ones by virtually every metric of wellbeing.
nodes.ref: Putnam, Bowling Alone (2000); Fukuyama, Trust (1995)

nodes.label: Tribalism
nodes.desc: The in-group/out-group distinction is cognitively automatic and cross-culturally universal. Social identity theory: we derive self-esteem from group membership.
nodes.ref: Tajfel & Turner, Social Identity Theory (1979); Haidt, The Righteous Mind (2012)

nodes.label: Cooperation
nodes.desc: Humans cooperate with non-kin strangers at an unparalleled scale — enabled by shared fictions, institutions, and the capacity for altruistic punishment of cheaters.
nodes.ref: Axelrod, The Evolution of Cooperation (1984); Henrich, The Secret of Our Success (2015)

nodes.label: Freedom
nodes.desc: Berlin's two concepts: negative liberty (freedom from interference) vs. positive liberty (capacity for self-realization). Both matter — and they conflict.
nodes.ref: Berlin, Two Concepts of Liberty (1958); Nozick vs. Rawls debate

nodes.label: Reciprocity
nodes.desc: The universal norm of returning benefit for benefit. Strong reciprocity — rewarding cooperators and punishing defectors at personal cost — stabilizes large-scale cooperation.
nodes.ref: Fehr & Gächter, Altruistic Punishment in Humans (2002, Nature)

nodes.label: Status
nodes.desc: Dominance hierarchies exist in all human societies. Relative status (not absolute wealth) is the strongest predictor of health outcomes — the social gradient of health.
nodes.ref: Marmot, The Status Syndrome (2004); Sapolsky, Behave (2017)

nodes.label: Evolution
nodes.desc: Darwin's insight: random variation plus natural selection, given deep time, generates all biological complexity without foresight or design.
nodes.ref: Darwin, On the Origin of Species (1859); Dawkins, The Selfish Gene (1976)

nodes.label: Life
nodes.desc: Life is defined by autopoiesis — self-maintenance through metabolism — and Darwinian heredity. Its origin from chemistry remains one of science's deepest open questions.
nodes.ref: Maturana & Varela, Autopoiesis and Cognition (1980); Szostak, The Origin of Life (2009)

nodes.label: Extinction
nodes.desc: 99.9% of all species that ever lived are extinct. We are likely in Earth's sixth mass extinction, the first driven by a single species.
nodes.ref: Kolbert, The Sixth Extinction (2014); Barnosky et al. (2011, Nature)

nodes.label: Symbiosis
nodes.desc: Cooperation between organisms of different species — from mitochondria (ancient endosymbiosis) to gut microbiome. The microbiome influences mood, immunity, and cognition.
nodes.ref: Margulis, Symbiosis in Cell Evolution (1981); Cryan et al., The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis (2019)

nodes.label: Chaos
nodes.desc: Deterministic systems can be unpredictable beyond short timescales due to sensitivity to initial conditions — the butterfly effect. Weather is the canonical example.
nodes.ref: Lorenz, Deterministic Nonperiodic Flow (1963); Gleick, Chaos (1987)

nodes.label: Space
nodes.desc: Space is not the backdrop of events but a dynamic field that bends under mass and expands at accelerating rates. We may inhabit one bubble in a multiverse.
nodes.ref: Einstein, General Relativity (1915); Guth, The Inflationary Universe (1997)

nodes.label: Silence
nodes.desc: In neuroscience, silence is not the absence of neural activity but an active state of the auditory cortex. Silence is as much constructed by the brain as sound.
nodes.ref: Bhikkhu, The Sounds of Silence (2010); Cage, 4'33" (1952)

nodes.label: Transcendence
nodes.desc: The experience of going beyond ordinary self-boundaries. Self-transcendent experiences activate default mode network suppression and increase a sense of unity.
nodes.ref: Yaden et al., The Varieties of Self-Transcendent Experience (2017, Review of General Psychology)

nodes.label: Meaning
nodes.desc: Frankl distinguished meaning from pleasure: meaning comes through suffering, work, and love. Neuroscience links meaning-making to the ventromedial prefrontal cortex.
nodes.ref: Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning (1946); Heintzelman & King, Life Is Pretty Meaningful (2014)

nodes.label: Impermanence
nodes.desc: The Buddhist insight that all phenomena are transient. Modern physics agrees: particles are excitations of fields, and fields themselves are impermanent configurations of energy.
nodes.ref: Thich Nhat Hanh, The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching (1998)

nodes.label: Sacred
nodes.desc: Durkheim: the sacred/profane distinction is the fundamental religious category. Something becomes sacred through collective ritual — it is a social, not divine, designation.
nodes.ref: Durkheim, The Elementary Forms of Religious Life (1912); Haidt, The Righteous Mind (2012)

nodes.label: Compassion
nodes.desc: Distinct from empathy: compassion is the motivational response to others' suffering. Cultivating compassion (vs. empathy alone) reduces burnout and increases helping behavior.
nodes.ref: Singer & Klimecki, Empathy versus Compassion (2014, Trends in Cognitive Sciences)

nodes.label: Interconnection
nodes.desc: The ecological, quantum, and philosophical insight that boundaries between self and world are porous — organisms are processes embedded in larger processes.
nodes.ref: Capra, The Web of Life (1996); Thich Nhat Hanh, Interbeing (1987)

nodes.label: Intention
nodes.desc: The bridge from thought to action. In behavioral science, intention is the proximal commitment that converts beliefs, norms, and perceived control into behavior.
nodes.ref: Ajzen, The Theory of Planned Behavior (1991); Bratman, Intention, Plans, and Practical Reason (1987)

nodes.label: Collective Intention
nodes.desc: Shared purposive orientation across many agents. Individual commitments become coordinated action when norms, identity, and common goals stabilize together.
nodes.ref: Searle, The Construction of Social Reality (1995); Tomasello, A Natural History of Human Thinking (2014)

nodes.label: Action
nodes.desc: The behavioral output of intention. Motor preparation, action selection, and inhibition link internal goals to observable conduct.
nodes.ref: Cisek & Kalaska, Neural Mechanisms for Interacting with a World Full of Action Choices (2010); Grafton, The Cognitive Neuroscience of Prehension (2010)

nodes.label: Moral Valence
nodes.desc: The polarity of an intention or act within a social-moral field. It captures whether a pathway is currently tuned toward harm, restraint, repair, or prosocial benefit.
nodes.ref: Greene, Moral Tribes (2013); Haidt, The Righteous Mind (2012)

nodes.label: Crime
nodes.desc: A harmful or norm-violating social outcome. Moral disengagement, low trust, status threat, and weak regulation can push intention toward antisocial action.
nodes.ref: Bandura, Moral Disengagement (2016); Agnew, General Strain Theory (1992)

nodes.label: Kindness
nodes.desc: Prosocial action directed toward others' wellbeing. Kindness emerges when empathy, regulation, and cooperative norms align into low-cost helping behavior.
nodes.ref: Curry et al., Is It Good to Be Good? (2018); Lyubomirsky et al., The Benefits of Frequent Positive Affect (2005)

nodes.label: Collective Identity
nodes.desc: The nation-scale or group-scale attractor in which shared symbols, norms, and intentions stabilize into durable identity, policy, and culture.
nodes.ref: Tajfel, Human Groups and Social Categories (1981); Anderson, Imagined Communities (1983)

nodes.label: Empathy / Compassion
nodes.desc: The affective and motivational tuner that can redirect intention away from harm and toward helping, repair, and cooperative concern.
nodes.ref: Singer & Klimecki, Empathy versus Compassion (2014); Decety & Cowell, The Complex Relation Between Morality and Empathy (2014)

nodes.label: Harm
nodes.desc: Damage, injury, or imposed suffering at the outcome layer. Harm is modeled as an externalized result shaped by intention, opportunity, norms, and restraint.
nodes.ref: World Health Organization violence frameworks; Galtung, Violence, Peace, and Peace Research (1969)

nodes.label: Distrust
nodes.desc: A breakdown in expected reciprocity and reliability. Distrust propagates across groups when fear, past conflict, and institutional weakness accumulate.
nodes.ref: Hardin, Trust and Trustworthiness (2002); Fukuyama, Trust (1995)

nodes.label: Fear Contagion
nodes.desc: The spread of threat perception through groups, media, and imitation. Fear contagion amplifies defensive coordination and can harden aggression.
nodes.ref: Hatfield, Cacioppo & Rapson, Emotional Contagion (1993); LeDoux, The Emotional Brain (1996)

nodes.label: Dehumanization
nodes.desc: The reduction of persons to less-than-human status, lowering moral restraint and widening tolerance for cruelty, exclusion, and coercion.
nodes.ref: Haslam, Dehumanization (2006); Bandura, Moral Disengagement (1999)

nodes.label: Fragmentation
nodes.desc: The splintering of a shared civic field into antagonistic blocs. Fragmentation weakens trust, coordination, and collective problem-solving.
nodes.ref: Putnam, Bowling Alone (2000); Sunstein, #Republic (2017)

nodes.label: Coercion
nodes.desc: The use of force, pressure, or threat to secure compliance. In the model, coercion rises when legitimacy falls and institutions rely on compulsion over consent.
nodes.ref: Weber, Politics as a Vocation (1919); Tyler, Why People Obey the Law (1990)

nodes.label: Exploitation
nodes.desc: One-sided extraction of value under asymmetry of power, knowledge, or vulnerability. Exploitation converts weak norms into unequal outcomes.
nodes.ref: Young, Responsibility for Justice (2011); Sen, Development as Freedom (1999)

nodes.label: Law
nodes.desc: Codified rules that translate collective norms into formal standards, sanctions, and rights. Law mediates between intention, institutions, and social outcomes.
nodes.ref: Hart, The Concept of Law (1961); Fuller, The Morality of Law (1964)

nodes.label: Education
nodes.desc: Structured transmission of knowledge, norms, and interpretive skill. Education shapes receiver quality, empathy thresholds, and civic cooperation.
nodes.ref: Dewey, Democracy and Education (1916); OECD social learning research

nodes.label: Incentive
nodes.desc: The reward or penalty structure that channels action tendencies toward cooperation, compliance, or extraction. Incentives filter intention into behavior.
nodes.ref: Kahneman & Tversky, Prospect Theory (1979); Ostrom, Governing the Commons (1990)

nodes.label: Historical Memory
nodes.desc: The stored residue of prior conflict, repair, victory, trauma, and institution-building. Historical memory conditions present trust and threat interpretation.
nodes.ref: Halbwachs, On Collective Memory (1950); Assmann, Cultural Memory (2011)
